text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum over the pelvic promontory, with pneumoperitoneum aiding in visualizing the air tracking behind the superior rectal artery. The proximity of the superior rectal artery to the left iliac vein is noted, especially in slim patients.

The surgeon carefully dissects the hypogastric nerves away from the back of the superior rectal artery. The camera provides a steady, static view, revealing the left iliac vein. Traction and counter-traction are applied by the assistant to facilitate this dissection.

The surgeon uses a monopolar scissor in a brush-like technique to perform medial dissection along the side of the inferior mesenteric artery, meticulously exposing each tissue layer. Traction is applied under the superior rectal artery using robotic arm III, while counter traction is provided by the assistant with a U-hand forceps.

The surgeon continues the dissection parallel to the inferior mesenteric artery, revealing the hypogastric nerve at the pelvic brim. As the dissection progresses, the left common iliac artery becomes visible, with meticulous dissection being performed around these structures.
